item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but it would have been nice to hear more about the dreams and what they entail. The final example was a nice touch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I do agree with the criticisms levelled at this article, I think it is still sufficiently interesting and engaging to warrant an upvote. I think more could be made of the connections between subject state and entity behaviour (i.e. independent and dependent variables) because as it is there are tantalising hints but not enough to draw any solid conclusions. I also didn't much care for the suicide and redaction at the end: that would be the place to put the final clues to allow the mystery of the SCP to come to light.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Have to agree with this. The main thing coming to my mind as an issue is that there is no real connection between the manifestations. Most seem to reflect the ink user's state of mind, but then there's the one that's suggested to be an image of the D-Class's victim. "That's a bad mirror" is a great line, but as psul said, has associated problems.

This also has the effect of making the description very bland. I'm reading about these things appearing behind a person and going, okay, what are they? Given that they're very individualized, the bulk of the description ends up in the testing logs; ergo, getting there faster is suggested.

You've also got some tone issues, like "betray no awareness" and "numerous reports of insanity" (or maybe it's just that I can't see that being a thing), and I had to look askance at them subjecting a D-Class to "45 minutes of psychological torture" just because. I mean, maybe it's because you used that exact phrase. The connection to the other SCPs linked in the article isn't apparent, and there's also what I said above, though at least these things are visible to others. (I would suggest dropping the lucid dream angle, since nothing is made of it, but I'm extremely biased against dream skips.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here are my comments as part of the Young and Under 30 Project.

What works well: The basic idea of "spooky thing that appears behind you" is fertile ground for skips, and the fact that the particular thing that each person sees reflects their mental state provides a narrative structure. The writing itself is technically fine for the most part.

What could be improved: This skip gets far too bogged down in the mechanics of the anomaly (how long it lasts, its link to the centre of mass), spending a lot of words on those things when they aren't critical to the image or the story of the skip. This is such a staple horror image that bringing an engaging approach to it is important, and this article does nothing much with the basic trope - you don't even give a vivid description of the apparitions.

Several things appear tacked on. I'm broadly okay with including an otherwise-irrelevant recovery log for the purposes of crosslinking, but the self-harm and suicide angle appears to be there just to add danger. The lucid dream angle is never followed up to any effect (you even mention a dream but don't go on to describe it). And the Foundation's torture of a political prisoner comes across as gratuitous.

On that note, the dialogue in the testing logs doesn't feel immersive - while interruptions and repetition is probably more realistic, it creates roadbumps for the reader which can jar them out of the story, and I think that's what happens here.

Finally, I am quite uncomfortable with your depiction of the psyche of a person with schizophrenia as a constantly changing shape with spooky red eyes - that comes across as a very cliched view of schizophrenia, and it's made worse by the self-harm at the end of the test.

How to improve it: If the narrative is intended to be a visual representation of the mind, streamline the skip to focus on that. Get rid of the description that is too focused on mechanics, and slim down the test logs to that effect as well as cutting unnecessary dialogue. Then work out what set of psyches you'd like to exhibit, and how to arrange them to give escalation across the tests.

Verdict: Downvoted. This is a solid basic concept that is tripping up over its execution.

The Young and under 30 project aims to bring attention to articles that are less than a year old, and have a score of less than +30. These are often the articles getting the least attention on site, and we want to change that!

Here's how you can help: read this article, vote, and comment on why you voted the way you did. Your comments don't have to be in this format - write it however you like. And if you use IRC, give this post a plug on the chat to get more commenters over here - you can find the direct link by clicking "Options" —> "Permanent link".

You don't have to wait for posts like this one - you can initiate discussion yourself on other Yau30 articles. A list of eligible articles can be found here. Remember to update the list to track any articles that you've reviewed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While the logs try to give us a glimpse into the action, they do not truly provide much new information to carry the article forward. It would be better if you relate how the manifestations to the person who evokes it, and derive some semblance of a story from there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How did this Series 1 skip wind up in Series 4!?

Seriously though, I liked it. The final expunged data is as much cheese as it is tease.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this. It's simple, straight forward, not trying to end the world. It's just an object that does weird things when you use it and can sometimes have a strong affect on it's users.
I'm surprised this isn't rated more highly, but it certainly gets my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This just reminds me of one of the 3000 contest entries (the one that manifests your fears to watch over you while you sleep), and I didn't particularly like that one as it is.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
I came up with this while writing it.

Thanks to FoldingPaper, 8ync, Salvagebar, Rimple, Scented_Shadow, and Jekeled for the feedback!

Check out the rest of the Linville Arc!

SCP-3066: Absurdity

SCP-3076: Avidity
